figures.caption: Table 8: Multiclass F1 on ARFBench across question categories and models. Invalid answers are randomly shuffled to incorrect answer classes and results are averaged over 10 random seeds where applicable. The best model performance in each metric is bolded. The second best model performance in each metric is underlined.
Figure 8: An example where ChatTS does not utilize the extra textual context given in the question to solve the problem.
Figure 12: Top models fail to use sufficient domain knowledge, in contrast to domain experts.

Figure 1: ARFBench consists of 750 question-answer (QA) pairs, derived from 63 real-world incidents and 142 observability time series. Observability time series are highly nonstationary and complex (Cohen et al., 2025), and ARFBench includes highly multivariate series that challenge LLM/VLM input representations. A. Workflow of ARFBench question-answer generation. Engineers use commercial messaging platforms to respond to incidents, where they typically send time series widgets that visualize relevant metrics. Time series and incident timelines from internally monitored incidents are used as input to an LLM pipeline and fit to eight different question templates testing various aspects of anomalies. The resulting multiple choice question-answer pairs can be used to evaluate various predictive models. B. ARFBench questions are designed to capture escalating levels of difficulty. C. Our model, Toto-1.0-QA-Experimental, obtains high accuracy and F1 on ARFBench, comparable to recent frontier models.

Figure 4: Architecture diagram of the Toto-1.0-QA-Experimental (Toto-Qwen3-VL) model. Frozen weights are denoted with a snowflake, while trainable weights are marked with a flame. Training a small set of weights allows us to adapt and augment the capabilities of either pretrained model.
Figure 11: Toto-1.0-Qwen3 32B does not correctly perceive the time series, missing the changing trend in the green channel.

Figure 7: Process of generating synthetic multivariate data for post-training VLMs, TSFM-VLMs, and TSFM-LLMs. Gaussian noise is transformed by adding seasonality and drift components, then anomalies are injected at randomly chosen points within the time series. To make the data more realistic, captions, channel names, and reasoning are generated with an LLM to reflect the observability setting.

Table 4: GPT-4o and GPT-5 accuracy and F1 on ARFBench when various parts of the input are ablated. No TS data means the timeseries image was not given to the model (i.e. the answers are only dependent on the time series description). No Caption means the time series description was not given to the model (i.e. the model only has the time series plot as input). Full input means both the description and plot were present. The best performing ablation for each model (excluding baselines) is highlighted for each question category.
Figure 15: The model reasons incorrectly, likely due to a lack of contextualization with the time series description.
Figure 6: Distribution of question categories (left) and time series domains (right) in ARFBench.
Table 6: Post-training hyperparameters for SFT and RL (DAPO) across model families.
Figure 10: Gemini 3 Pro correctly perceives the graph, but reverses the cause and effect, which is a lack of domain knowledge error. 28

Figure 2: Example questions in ARFBench for each tier. For each question, the model is given a time series caption along with corresponding time series data in image or tabular form. In addition to the input above, time series channels are named with tags that represent collections such as datacenters, pods, services, and more.

Table 5: An overview of the question categories within ARFBench. Questions are ordered by increasing difficulty, corresponding to the required reasoning and synthesis across all given inputs. Each increasing tier of difficulty requires the correct reasoning of the previous lower tier.

Figure 5: Top: Example metric query used to query the time series database. The blue text represents the metric name. The green text represents the filters. The red text represents space aggregation functions and group-by keys. Finally, the purple text represents a time aggregation function. Bottom: Example summarized time series description of the metric query above.
Figure 14: Experts also make some understanding and/or instruction-following errors which models often do not make.
Figure 9: An example where GPT-4.1 (VLM) lacks the domain knowledge and perceptual ability to notice an anomaly.

Table 7: Accuracy on ARFBench across question categories and models. For Random Choice only, invalid answers are randomly shuffled to incorrect answer classes. The best model performance in each metric is bolded. The second best model performance in each metric is underlined. Our model, Toto-1.0-QAExperimental, achieves the top accuracy among models tested.

Table 3: Accuracy and multiclass macro-F1 on ARFBench, sorted by model type and accuracy. Bootstrapped 95% CIs resampled over 1000 batches of nbootstrap = 750 are provided for overall accuracy and F1. The best model score in each metric is bolded. The second best model score in each metric is underlined.
Figure 13: Experts do not always perceive fine-grained details about exact timings of when anomalies start or end, leading to perceptual errors, in contrast to models.

Figure 3: ARFBench requires multivariate reasoning. A single time series variate taken out of context may not appear to be anomalous. However, in a structured multivariate grouping, the variate would be considered anomalous. The time series shown above is based on average TLS certificate lifetime across cluster IDs.
